revision.comment: expanded with information about the shrine's mythology
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo Jingu Honden 2008.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main shrine | width = | type = | dedication = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Amenooshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]]
[[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | phone = | website = http://www.udojingu.com/ }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is dedicated to [[Ugayafukiaezu]], [[Amaterasu]], [[Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], [[Hoori]] and [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Shrine legend== The temple dates to 30 BC, during the reign of Emperor Sujin. Udo shrine is located near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is found inside a cavern with a view of the ocean. Within the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription needed|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Commons category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]]

It is dedicated to [[Ugayafukiaezu]], [[Amaterasu]], [[Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], [[Hoori]] and [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Shrine legend== Udo shrine is located near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is found inside a cavern with a view of the ocean. Within the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription needed|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Commons category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]]

[[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | phone = | website = http://www.udojingu.com/ }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is dedicated to [[Ugayafukiaezu]], [[Amaterasu]], [[Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], [[Hoori]] and [[Emperor Jimmu]]. ==Shrine legend== Udo shrine is located near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is found inside a cavern with a view of the ocean. Within the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription needed|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Commons category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo Jingu Honden 2008.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main shrine | width = | type = | dedication = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Amenooshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]]
[[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | phone = | website = http://www.udojingu.com/ }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father Yamasachihiko, dedicated to [[Ugayafukiaezu]], [[Amaterasu]], [[Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], [[Hoori]] and Emperor Jimmu. The shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. ==Shrine legend== Udo shrine is located in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is found inside a cavern with a view of the ocean. [[Hoori]]

It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&pg=PA307&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&hl=en&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&source=gb_mobile_search&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwidzrDW7Ib0AhVYmHIEHfMYAh4Q6AF6BAgEEAM|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription needed|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Shinto shrine}} ==References== {{Commons category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]]

[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] ==References== {{Commons category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Udo Jingū]]==References== {{Commons category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Udo Jingū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Udo Jingū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Udo Jingū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]]

The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Udo Jingū]]==References== {{Commons category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[en:Udo Jingū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[en:Udo Jingū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]] [[en:Udo Jingū]]

[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. 鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]

It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. [[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Udo Jingū]] == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Udo Jingū]] == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Udo Jingū]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Munakata Faith]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Udo Jingū]] [[sn:Udo Jingū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}}

Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Udo Jingū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Udo Jingū]] [[Category:Munakata Faith]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Udo Jingū]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Udo Jingū]] [[sn:Udo Jingū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Udo-jingu}} [[Category:New]]

Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[Miyazaki prefecture]], [[Japan]], south of [[:en:Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:Munakata Faith]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Udo Jingū]] [[en:Udo Jingū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Udo-jingu}} [[de:Udo-Schrein]] [[fr:Udo-jingū]] [[ja:鵜戸神宮]] [[ko:우도 신궁]] [[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[:en:Cormorant|Cormorant]]. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. revision.text:
[[sn:commons:Category:Udo-jingu]][[de:Udo-Schrein]][[en:Udo Jingū]][[fr:Udo-jingū]][[ja:鵜戸神宮]][[ko:우도 신궁]][[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]][[zh:鵜戶神宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q704430]] Wikidata name: Udo Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[:en:Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], south of [[:en:Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[:en:Toyotamahime|Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[:en:Cormorant|Cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] }} [[en:Udo Jingū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Udo-jingu}} [[de:Udo-Schrein]] [[fr:Udo-jingū]] [[ja:鵜戸神宮]] [[ko:우도 신궁]] [[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]] {{wikidata link|Q704430}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q704430}}

The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. [[commons:Category:Udo-jingu]][[de:Udo-Schrein]][[en:Udo Jingū]][[fr:Udo-jingū]][[ja:鵜戸神宮]][[ko:우도 신궁]][[zh_yue:鵜戶神宮]][[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[commons:Category:Udo-jingu]][[de:Udo-Schrein]][[en:Udo Jingū]][[fr:Udo-jingū]][[ja:鵜戸神宮]][[ko:우도 신궁]][[zh_yue:鵜戶神宮]][[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[sn:commons:Category:Udo-jingu]][[de:Udo-Schrein]][[en:Udo Jingū]][[fr:Udo-jingū]][[ja:鵜戸神宮]][[ko:우도 신궁]][[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]][[zh:鵜戶神宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q704430]] Wikidata name: Udo Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Miyazaki Prefecture, Japan}} {{one source|date=November 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Udo-jingū
鵜戸神宮 | image = Udo-jingu Main hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' (main shrine) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|31|39|01|N|131|28|00|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ugayafukiaezu]]
[[Amaterasu]]
[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]]
[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hoori]]
[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, [[:en:Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Nichinan, Miyazaki|Nichinan]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], south of [[:en:Aoshima, Miyazaki|Aoshima]]. It is the mythical birthplace of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[:en:Toyotamahime|Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[:en:Cormorant|Cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[en:Udo Jingū]] {{Commons category|Udo-jingu}} [[de:Udo-Schrein]] [[fr:Udo-jingū]] [[ja:鵜戸神宮]] [[ko:우도 신궁]] [[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]] {{wikidata link|Q704430}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Udo Jingū]] {{Commons category|Udo-jingu}} [[de:Udo-Schrein]] [[fr:Udo-jingū]] [[ja:鵜戸神宮]] [[ko:우도 신궁]] [[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]] {{wikidata link|Q704430}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is the mythical birthplace of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[:en:Toyotamahime|Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a [[:en:Cormorant|Cormorant]]. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Udo Jingū]] {{Commons category|Udo-jingu}} [[de:Udo-Schrein]] [[fr:Udo-jingū]] [[ja:鵜戸神宮]] [[ko:우도 신궁]] [[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]] {{wikidata link|Q704430}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3232, Miyaura, {{ill|Nichinan, Miyazaki|en|Nichinan, Miyazaki|WD=Q860669|lt=Nichinan}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki}} 887-0101 | website = {{URL|http://www.udojingu.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Udo-jingū'''|鵜戸神宮|}} is a Shinto shrine in {{ill|Nichinan, Miyazaki|en|Nichinan, Miyazaki|WD=Q860669|lt=Nichinan}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, south of {{ill|Aoshima, Miyazaki|en|Aoshima, Miyazaki|WD=Q4778779|lt=Aoshima}}. It is the mythical birthplace of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}'s father [[Ugayafukiaezu]].{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Udo-jing%C5%AB&pg=PA307|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=307|language=en}} According to shrine legends, it is the place where the sea goddess [[:en:Toyotamahime|Toyotamahime]], the mother of said Ugayafukiaezu, built a birth-hut from the feathers of a {{ill|Cormorant|en|Cormorant|WD=Q3901247|lt=Cormorant}}. Other gods venerated here are Yamasachihiko (alias Hohodemi alias [[Hoori]], Jinmu's grandfather), [[Amaterasu]], {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Amenooshihomimi}}, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], and Emperor Jimmu. While the original myth includes a tragic divorce of Ugayafukiaezu's parents, the shrine is popular with young couples hoping for easy childbirth and a happy marriage. == Local characteristics == Udo shrine is in a cave in the side of the cliff, near the Nichinan coast of Miyazaki. The ''honden'', or main shrine, is in a cavern with a view of the ocean. In the cave is the ''ochichi iwa'', or "breast stone," a dripping stone which is said to have fed the kami Ugayafukiaezu, father of the first emperor of Japan, when his mother returned to the sea. The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Udo Jingū]] {{Commons category|Udo-jingu}} [[de:Udo-Schrein]] [[fr:Udo-jingū]] [[ja:鵜戸神宮]] [[ko:우도 신궁]] [[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]] {{wikidata link|Q704430}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The shrine sells a candy made from the water of this stone, ''mizuame'', a kind of taffy.{{cite book|last1=Dougill|first1=John|last2=Cali|first2=Joseph|title=Shinto shrines : a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|location=Honolulu|isbn=9780824837136|url=http://muse.jhu.edu/books/9780824837754}}{{subscription required|via=[https://muse.jhu.edu/ Project MUSE]}} In the slight distance of the cavern is a rocky island with two peaks. Attendees can purchase small clay balls and attempt to toss them between the peaks for good luck. == References == {{Commons Category|Udo-jingu|Udo-jingū}} {{Reflist}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Udo Jingū]] {{Commons category|Udo-jingu}} [[de:Udo-Schrein]] [[fr:Udo-jingū]] [[ja:鵜戸神宮]] [[ko:우도 신궁]] [[zh:鵜戶神宮]] [[zh-yue:鵜戶神宮]] {{wikidata link|Q704430}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Miyazaki prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Nichinan, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
